text: BLIN (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. of Chaumont. The cant. comprises 15 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 5,807.—The com. is 24 m. NE of Chaumont. Pop. 592.
BLINDBOTHEL, a township in the p. of Brigham, in Cumberland, 2 m. W of Cockermouth. Pop. 100.
BLINDCRAKE, a township in the p. of Isell, in Cumberland, 3 m. NNE of Cockermouth. Pop. with Redmain and Isell, 347.
BLIND HARBOUR, a small bay on the coast of co. Cork, 3 m. ENE of Castlehaven harbour.

BLINK KLIP, or SENSAVAN, a town and fort of S Africa, in the Griquas territory, 60 m. SSW of Kruman. BLIS, BELES, or BALES, a town of Syria, in the pashalik of Damascus, on the r. bank of the Euphrates, 45 m. E of Rakka, and 52 m. ESE of Aleppo. From this place downwards the river improves considerably, exchanging its shingle islands and shoals, for sandy islands with bluff terminations, and bold shores thickly covered with tamarisks and poplars, as are also the banks with little exception as far down as Anna, a distance of 425 m. It has been suggested that B. might make an efficient terminus for the voyage upwards of steamers, as a postroad or even railroad might be easily carried from it across a perfectly level country to Aleppo, and the journey performed in country, the arrond. of B. comprises the district of 5 or 6 hours. From Port William on the Aleppo side of the town of Bir, about 3 m. lower down the stream, to B., the distance by the river is 101 m.
BLISE. See BLIES.
BLISE-BRUCKEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, and cant. of Sarreguemines, on the Blise. Pop. 1,705.
BLISLAND, a parish in Cornwall, 4 m. NNW of Bodmin. Area 6,800 acres. Pop. 688.

BLISSFIELD, a village of Lenawee co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., in the township of the same name, on the N bank of the Raisin, on the Erie and Kalamazoo railroad, 71 m. S of Detroit. Pop. in 1840, 778.

BLISWORTH, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. NW of Castlethorpe. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 882. At this point the Northampton and Peterborough railway runs off from the London and Birmingham line. The Grand Junction canal passes through a tunnel in the vicinity of the v.; and the London and Birmingham railway is conducted through a very deep cutting here, which, though not the largest work of the kind on the line, has, from the character of the material, been by far the most expensive and arduous. The Tring cutting contains a greater cubic content, but the material being entirely chalk, much less difficulty was experienced in the execution than in the B., which consists chiefly of a hard blue limestone belonging to the oolitic series, and which yielded throughout all seasons large quantities of water, which it was necessary to drain off by pumping. The working of the rock was also rendered more difficult and tedious by the strata being interstratified by beds of blue shale, which being impervious to water, rendered every means of drawing it off, except that of pumping, unavailable. The B. excavation contains 1,200,000 cubic yards, averaging 50 ft. deep, for 2 m. in length. About 400,000 cubic yards were removed from each end to form the adjoining embankments, which reach the height of 45 ft. at the highest point. The remaining 400,000 were raised up the steep side of the excavation, and deposited on the adjoining lands, forming what are termed sprit banks. The cost of this work was £200,000.
BLITAR, a town of the island of Java, in the Asiatic archipelago, in the prov. of Brindiok, 72 m. SW of Surabaya. In the environs are the ruins of Penataran.
BLITHE. See BLYTH.
BLITHFIELD, a parish in Staffordshire, 3½ m. N of Rugeley. Area 4,180 acres. Pop., with the hamlets of Admaston and Newton, 390.
BLO'-NORTON, or Norton Belleau, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. S of East Harling. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 435.

BLOCK ISLAND, or Manisses, an island belonging to the state of Rhode island, U.S., 14 m. SSW of Point Judith, and 24 m. SW of Newport. It is 8 m. in length; and varies from 2 m. to 4 m. in breadth; and forms the township of Newshoreham, in Newport co. It presents a generally hilly surface, and contains several small lakes or ponds. The soil, consisting of sand and gravelly loam, is fertile. It was formerly celebrated for its cattle and dairies; fishing now constitutes the chief employment of the inhabitants. The cap. is Shoreham. Pop. 722.

BLOCKLEY, a parish of Worcestershire, but locally in Gloucestershire, 3 m. NW of Morton-in- Marsh. Area 7,660 acres. Pop. 2,136, of whom 1,412 were in the township of B. It comprises the hamlets of Aston-Magna, Dorne, Ditchford, Draycott, and Paxford. There are several silk-mills in this p.

BLOCKLEY, a township of Philadelphia co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S. It slopes to the S, and is watered by Mill and Cobb creeks, and the Schuylkill. Its soil is generally sandy loam. BLO BLOCKZYL. See BLOKZYL.
BLODELSHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Upper Rhine, cant. of Ensisheim. Pop. 1,778.
BLOEMBERG, a mountain of S Africa, in S lat. 30° 58′, E long. 19° 52′, near the sources of the Doorne.
BLOEMENDAAL, a village of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, 2 m. NNW of Haarlem. Pop. 1,100.—Also a v. in the prov. of S Holland, 2 m. NNW of Gouda.

BLOEMENDAL, a village of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, a little to the NNE of Haarlem. It has manufactories of linen, and spinning-mills. The environs contain extensive nursery-gardens. Pop. 1,200.
BLOEM'S FOUNTAIN, a village of S Africa, in the Griquas territory, 5 m. S of Blink Klip, and 30 m. N of Griquatown, or Klaarwater.
BLOFIELD, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. W by S of Acle. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 1,112.

BLOIS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and city of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher.—The arrond. has an area of 250,397 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 118,561; in 1846, 128,587. It comprises 9 cant., viz. Saint-Aignan, B., Bracieux, Contres, Herbault, Marchenoir, Mer, Montrichard, and Ouzouerle-Marche.—The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 24,855.—The city of B., the cap. of the ancient Blaisois, and now of the dep. of Loir-et- Cher, is situated on the Loire, in N lat. 47° 35′ 20″, E long. 1° 17'; 96 m. direct distance SW by S of Paris, 105 m. by road; 36 m. SW of Orleans; on the line of the Paris and Tours railway. Alt. above the Loire 80 metres, or 262 ft.; above sea-level, 102 metres, or 334 ft. Pop. in 1789, 9,646; in 1821, 13,085; in 1836, 13,628; in 1846, 15,900. It occupies a picturesque site on the declivity of a hill, on the r. or N bank of the Loire, which is here crossed by a stone bridge of 11 arches, having a pyramid in the middle 60 ft. high, uniting the city with the suburb of Vienne. The upper part of the town is the most ancient. It was greatly improved by the dukes of Orleans; but its structure does not equal the beauty of its situation, the streets being narrow, and many of the houses low. A fine quay lines the banks of the river. The castle is celebrated as being the birth-place of the prince of Orleans, afterwards Louis XII., and the scene of the death of Catherine de Medici, and of the assassination of the duke of Guise, and his brother the cardinal, by order of Henry on 23d Dec., 1587. It stands on a rock overhanging the river, and has evidently been built to defend the place. It is now undergoing restoration; but a portion of it is still used as barracks. [Athenœum 1847, p. 623]. The fountains in various parts of the town are supplied with water from an aqueduct cut through the naked rock, supposed to have been the work of the Romans. The principal public buildings are the cathedral; the Jesuit's college, -now a communal college; and the episcopal palace, now the hotel de la prefecture, which presents curious specimens of different styles of architecture, and the terrace of which affords a pleasant walk. There is a public library of 19,000 vols. The trade of B. consists in wine, brandy, vinegar, corn, wood, and fruit; there are manufactures of serges, hosiery, and other cloths, gloves, leather, cutlery, hardware, and glass. B. is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Paris, whose diocese comprises the dep. of Loir-et-Cher.—The surrounding country is fertile and agreeable; and the prospect from the hill, on which the city stands, is that of a rich and highly cultivated country, with a noble river gliding majestically through it. Inglis says that B. and its neighbourhood are "colonized by English." -B. is a town of undoubted antiquity, although its name does not occur in the Pentingerian tables, nor in any of the ancient Itineraries. Gregory of Tours first notices it, in the 6th cent., under the name Blesæ. The counts of B. commence with William, who was killed in 834. The country was united to the Crown by Louis XII. In the 16th cent., the city was frequently the royal residence; and it sustained two sieges in 1577 and 1588. In 1814, when the allies approached Paris, the empress Marie- Louise retired to B.; and the last acts of the regency were dated from this city.—B. was the birth-place of Denis Papin, Jean Morin, Jean Bernier, and the Marquis de Favras.—Bernier, Hist. de B. 1682, 4to. —De la Saussaye, Hist. du chateau de B. 1840, 4to.— Inglis' Switzerland and the S of France.
BLOKULLA. See BLAKULLEN.

BLOKZYL, or BLOCZEIL, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, 19 m. NNW of Zwolle, on the Zuydersee, at the embouchure of the Steenwyk Aa. Pop. 1,570. It has a good harbour, and an active coasting-trade; and exports large quantities of butter.

BLOMBERG, or Blumberg, a village of Baden, in the Lake circle, 12 m. NNW of Schaffhausen. Pop. 594.—Also a town of Lippe-Detmold, 10 m. E of Detmold, on the Distel. Pop. 1,900. There is an ancient castle here, belonging to the princes of Lippe Schaumburg.
BLONAY, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district of Vevay. Pop. 861. There are mineral springs here, and an ancient castle.
BLOND, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. and 6 m. SSW of Bellac. Pop. 2,260.
BLONDE-FONTAINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Jussey. Pop. 1,103.

BLONIE, a town of Russian-Poland, in the gov. of Mazovia, 20 m. W of Warsaw. Pop. 1,000. It is a celebrated locality in the history of the wars of the Swedes and Poles.—Also a village in the same gov., 13 m. from Leczyca. Pop. 450.
BLOOD INDIANS, a native tribe of North American Indians, whose territory lay to the N of that of the Blackfoot tribe, between Battle river and the Saskatchewine, in about N lat. 55° 20'.

BLOODY FALL, a rapid, or shelving cascade, in Coppermine river, in N lat. 67° 42′ 35″, W long. 115° 49' 33". It is 300 yards in length, and has a descent of from 10 to 15 ft. It is between high banks of red tenacious clay, upon which rests a series of lofty green hills. Its distance from the mouth of the river is 11 m., through a country nearly level. The river, for 40 m. above it, flows over an unesen stony bed, full of rapids, so that it is impracticable to ascend it in boats drawing above a few inches of water.—Franklin.—Richardson.
BLOODY FORLAND, a promontory on the coast of co. Donegal, 3 m. W by S of Innisboffin, in N lat. 55° 10', W long. 8° 11'.
BLOODY POINT, a promontory on the SW side of the island of St. Kitt's.
BLOODY RUN, a village in Providence township, in Bedford co., Pennsylvania, U. S., 95 m. W by S of Harrisburg, on the S side of Royston branch of Juniata river.

BLOOM, a township in Seneca co., in the state of Ohio, 86 m. N of Columbus, on Honey creek. Pop. 1,168.—Also a township in Columbia co., in Pennsylvania, 10 m. NE of Danville, watered by the Susquehanna and Fishing creek. Pop. 1,174.—Also a township in Morgan co., in Ohio, on the Muskingum. Pop. 1,388.—Also a township in Sciota co., in Ohio. Pop. 913.—Also a township in Fairfield co., Ohio. BLO Pop. 2,301.—Also a township in Wood co., Ohio. Pop. 398.

BLOOMFIELD, a township in Somerset co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 35 m. N of Augusta, on the Kennebec. Pop. 1,093. - Also a township in Essex co., in Vermont, 90 m. NE of Montpelier, on the W side of Connecticut river. Pop. 179.—Also a township in Hartford co., in Connecticut, 7 m. NW of Hartford, on Wood river. Pop. 986.—Also a township in Essex co., in New Jersey, 54 m. NE of Trenton. Pop. 2,528.—Also a v. in Bloomfield township, in Crawford co., in Pennsylvania, 243 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. 564.—Also a v. in Perry co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 412.—Also a township in Knox co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,251.—Also a township in La Grange co., in Iowa. Pop. 307.—Also a town in Greene co., in Iowa, on the E side of the W branch of White river, 74 m. SW of Indianapolis. Pop. 700.—Also a v. in Stoddard co., Missouri, 233 m. SE of Jefferson.—Also a township in Oakland co., in Michigan, on the N branch of Rouge river. Pop. 1,508.—Also a township in Trumbull co., in Ohio, 15 m. N of Warren. Pop. 554.—Also a township in Richland co., in Ohio, 15 m. SE of Mansfield. Pop. 1,294.—Also a township in Logan co., in Ohio. Pop. 565.—Also a township in Jackson co., in Ohio, on Racoon creek. Pop. 721.—Also a v. in the township of Athol, in Upper Canada. Pop. 100.
BLOOMINGBURG, a village in Mamakatong township, Sullivan co., in the state of New York, U. S., 23 m. W of Newburgh. Pop. 500.—Also a v. in Fayette co., Ohio, 45 m. SSW of Columbus.
BLOOMINGDALE, a village in Jefferson co., in Ohio, 124 m. ENE of Columbus.—Also a v. in Dupage co., Illinois, 211 m. NNE of Springfield.
BLOOMINGGROVE, a township in Orange co., in the state of New York, U. S., 94 m. S of Albany. Pop. 2,396.—Also a township in Franklin co., in Iowa, 79 m. SE of Indianapolis.

BLOOMINGTON, the cap. of Monroe co., in the state of Iowa, 49 m. SW of Indianapolis, 616 m. from Washington. Pop. 1,550. The Indiana state university was founded here in 1827. It has 6 professors; and in 1847 was attended by 183 students. -Also a village in Maclean co., in Illinois, 73 m. NNE of Springfield.—Also a v. in Muscatine co., in Iowa, 320 m. above St. Louis, on the W bank of the Mississippi. Pop. 600.—Also a township in Macon co., in Missouri, 6 m. E of Chariton river.—Also a v. in Van Buren co., in Arkansas, 110 m. N of Little Rock.—Also a township in Buchanan co., in Missouri. Pop. 922.
BLOOMINGVILLE, a village in Erie co., in Ohio, 121 m. N by E of Columbus.
BLOOMSBURG, a village in Columbia co., Pennsylvania, 87 m. N by E of Harrisburg, on the W side of the Susquehanna. Pop. 600.—Also a v. in Halifax co., in Virginia, 140 m. SW by W of Richmond.
BLOOMSBURY, a village in Warren co., in the state of New Jersey, on both sides of Musconetcong creek, 43 m. NNW of Trenton.
BLOOMSVILLE, a village in Seneca co., in Ohio, 86 m. N of Columbus.
BLOOMVILLE, a village in Delaware co., in the state of New York, 74 m. SW of Albany, on the W branch of the Delaware. Pop. 150.
BLOORE-IN-TYRLEY, a township in the p. of Drayton-in-Hales, in Staffordshire, 3 m. E of Drayton. Pop., with the township of Hales, 561.

BLORE, a parish in Staffordshire, 3 m. NW of Ashborne, on the W bank of the Dove. Area 3,730 acres. Pop. 333. Bloreheath was, in 1459, the scene of a sanguinary conflict between the partizans of the houses of York and Lancaster.
BLOSKUL, a mountain of Bavaria, in the Pfalz circle, one of the loftiest summits of the Vosges or Wasgau.

BLOSSBURG, a village in Tioga co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 133 m. NNW of Harrisburg. In its vicinity is a rich bituminous coal mine. A railroad connects this place with Corning in the state of New York, whence there is a continuous lake and canal navigation to Albany.
BLOSSEVILLE - BONSECOURS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Boos, 13 m. SE of Rouen. Pop. 1,044. There are cotton manufactories here.
BLOSVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Sainte-Mère-Eglise. Pop. 401.
BLOT-L'EGLISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Menat. Pop. 1,168.
BLOTZHEIM, or BLADOTSHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Huningue. Pop. 2,287. There are mineral springs here.

BLOUNT, a county in the N part of the state of Alabama, U.S., on the head-streams of the Black Warrior. Area 1,650 sq. m. The surface is rolling, and the soil poor. Tobacco, cotton, Indian corn, wheat, and potatoes, are raised. The cap. is Blountsville. Pop. in 1840, 5,570, of whom 344 were slaves. -Also a county in the E part of the state of Tennessee. Area 900 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 11,745, of whom 945 were slaves. The cap. is Marysville.
BLOUNT SPRINGS, a village in Blount co., in Alabama, U. S., 88 m. NE of Tuscaloosa.

BLOUNTSVILLE, a village in Jones co., in Georgia, U. S., 16 m. W of Milledgeville.—Also a town in Blount co., Alabama, on the W side of Locustfork, a branch of the Black Warrior river, 104 m. NE of Tuscaloosa. Pop. 400.—Also a village in Sullivan co., in Tennessee, 283 m. E by N of Nashville.—Also a village in Henry co., in Iowa, 72 m. E by N of Indianapolis.
BLOUSTRAD, a village of Denmark, in Seeland, in the amt of Frederiksborg, 15 m. NNW of Copenhagen.
BLOWICK (HIGHER and Lower), two hamlets in the p. of North Meols, in Lancashire.
BLOWLY, a township in Llangadfan p., in Montgomeryshire. Pop. 85.
BLOXHAM, or BLOXHOLME, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. N by W of Sleaford. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 67.—Also a p. in Oxfordshire, 4 m. SW of Banbury. Area 4,240 acres. Pop. 1,543.
BLOXWICH, a village in the p. of Walsall, in Staffordshire, 2 m. NNE of Walsall.
BLOXWORTH, a parish in Dorsetshire, 6 m. NNW of Wareham. Area 3,420 acres. Pop. 306.
BLUBBERHOUSES, a township in the p. of Fewston, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. N by W of Otley. Area 3,600 acres. Pop. 99.
BLUCHER, a village of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, on the Sude river, SE of Boisenburg.
BLUCHER. See BUDERICH.
BLUDENTZ, a village of Austria, in the Tyrol, in the circle and 26 m. S of Bregenz, on the r. bank of the Ill. Pop. 1,865.
BLUDOWITZ, a village of Austria, in Silesia, in the circle and 9 m. W of Teschen, on the Luczona. Pop. 1,700.
BLUE BALL, a village in Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 47 m. SE of Harrisburg .- Also a v. in Cecil co., Maryland, 81 m. NE of Annapolis.
BLUEBERRY HILLS, a range in Saguenay co., in Lower Canada.

BLUE EARTH, or TERRE BLUE, a river of N America, rising in the Coteaux des Prairies, flowing NNE through the Sioux territory, and falling T. CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CO BLU into the St. Peter's river on the r. bank, about 60 m. above its junction with the Mississippi. It is the principal tributary of the St. Peter, and is said to furnish about two-thirds as much water as the main branch. Its name is a translation of the name given it by the Dacotas, who reside at its mouth, Makato Osa Watapa, i. e., 'the River where blue earth is gathered.' This earth is perhaps a phosphate of iron.
BLUE FELD'S BAY, a bay and village on the SW side of the island of Jamaica, in N lat. 18° 10' 30", W long. 78°. A mountain-peak near B. attains an alt. of 2,560 ft.
BLUEFIELDS. See BLEWFIELDS.
BLUE GRASS, a river in the state of Indiana, U. S., a tributary of the Great Wabash.
BLUE HILL, a township and v. in Hancock co., in Maine, 75 m. E of Augusta. Pop. 1,891. The v. is situated on the coast, 12 m. from Penobscot bay.

BLUE HILLS, a range of hills in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., traversing the counties of Nottingham, Barrington, and Rochester.—Also a range sighted by Captain Parry in Melville island, in N lat. 75° 30'.
BLUE ISLAND, a village in Cook co., in Illinois, 200 m. NNE of Springfield.
BLUE LAKE, a lake in the state of Illinois, in N lat. 41° 35', on the high grounds which divide the tributaries of the Illinois flowing SW, from the waters flowing NE into Lake Michigan.
BLUE LICK, a village in Clark co., in Iowa,

BLUE MOUNDS, a series of remarkable elevations on the NE part of Iowa co., in the state of Iowa, U. S., about 12 m. S of the Wisconsin river, and 30 m. W of Madison. They attain an alt. of 1,101 ft. above the level of the Wisconsin at Arena. U. S., 112 m. S by E of Indianapolis.

BLUE MOUNTAINS, a mass of mountains occupying nearly the whole of the E part of the island of Jamaica. The central and principal ridge, called Coldridge, runs nearly E and W, and varies from 5,000 to 8,000 ft. in alt. Another ridge, running across the island from SE to NW, forms the W buttress of this mountain-mass. On the S coast these mountains approach the sea in gigantic spurs of rapid acclivity, and thickly covered with timber; on the opposite side of the island they assume a softer character, and are separated by broad fertile valleys, while a level plain, in some places 12 m. in breadth, extends between their base and the sea. Blue Mountain Peak has an alt. of 7,770 ft.; Portland Gap, 5,640 ft.; Catherine's Peak, 4,970 ft. It has been asserted that three peaks of the Coldridge attain respectively 8,184, 7,656, and 7,576 ft.; but these admeasurements have not been verified.

BLUE MOUNTAINS, a range of Eastern Australia, which extends from the Warragong Mountains, or Australian Alps, on the S, to the Liverpool range on the N, running at a greater or less distance from the sea, to which it approaches, on the parallel of Sydney, within 40 m., and in some places, as at Illawarra and Newcastle, throwing out spurs which run down to the shore. It has an average alt. of 3,000 ft. The first endeavours of the colonists to penetrate beyond these mountains to the country on the W were so frequently defeated, that a period of 25 years passed away ere a pass was discovered through what had long appeared an impregnable barrier. This was at last accomplished in 1813; and in the following year a practicable line of road was constructed, by convict labour, from Emu ford, on the Nepean river, to Bathurst plains. The ascent as far as Springwood, 12½ m., is amid scenery which may remind the traveller of some of the passes of the Alps; and the mountains are well-clothed with timber, chiefly Euca- 3 D lypti and Casuarinæ; but beyond this to King's Table- | land, the summit of the road, alt. 3,400 ft. [Mitchell] they are barren; and, covered only with a thick brush interspersed here and there with a few stunted gumtrees, rise in "grand and dreary monotony of form and colour." [Meredith.] The rock is here coarse ferruginous sandstone, exposed in all parts, and intersected by ravines "deep in proportion to the height of the surface." Along the side of York mountain [alt. 3,292 ft.] the road winds terrace-wise, and is barely wide enough to allow of two vehicles passing. Hence the road descends, by the pass of Mount Victoria, at an inclination of 1 in 15, into the fine vale of Clwydd, across which it is carried by an arch and embankment. At York mountain, which is 62 m. from Sydney, the sandstone formation ceases, and a primitive formation, composed of quartz, granitic, and syenitic rocks, commences, which continues to Bathurst. From Cox's river, which flows through a bed of trap and granite [Mitchell], the road abruptly ascends Mount Lamby, for a distance of 7 m.; and then, crossing Stony range, passes over a series of undulating plains to Bathurst, 120 m. from Sydney, and 1,970 ft. above sea-level.

BLUE MOUNTAINS, an intermediate range between the Rocky and the Cascade mountains in Oregon. The N part is known as the Peak mountains. The S section divides into two spurs; the E joining the Bear mountains, and connected with the Wahsatch chain which enters California, and the W running towards Mount Maclaughlin in the Cascade range. Several transverse ranges unite the Blue with the Rocky mountains.
BLUE MOUNTAINS. See ALLEGHANY MOUNTAINS.
BLUE MOUNTAINS. See NALLA MALLA.
BLUE RIVER. See ABAI.

BLUE RIVER, a river of Indiana, U. S., rising in Washington co., and flowing in a SSW course to the Ohio, which it joins on the r. bank, a little above Frederica. It is sometimes called the Big Blue, to distinguish it from a small stream of the same name, which also joins the Ohio, on the same bank, 11 m. further down.
BLUE RIVER, a township in Hancock co., in the state of Iowa, U.S. Pop. 731.—Also a v. in Iowa co., in Wisconsin territory, 72 m. SSW of Madison. -Also a township in Harrison co., in Iowa. Pop. 1,429.
BLUE ROCK, a township in Muskingum co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., 65 E of Columbus, on both sides of Muskingum river. Pop. 1,083.
BLUE SEA, a name sometimes given to that portion of the sea of Corea which lies between the S coast of the peninsula and the island of Formosa.
BLUE SPRING, a township in Stewart co., in the state of Tennessee, 61 m. WNW of Nashville.

BLUESTACK, the central and highest summit of a triple-headed range of mountain, in co. Donegal. The other summits are Croghnagur on the E, and Silverhill on the W; and the range extends from Glenties to the gap of Barnsmore. B. has an alt. of 2,213 ft. above sea-level; and is the loftiest summit in a considerable extent of the Donegal highlands.

BLUE SULPHUR SPRINGS, a village in Green Briar co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 227 m. N by W of Richmond, much resorted to as a wateringplace, on account of the springs which have conferred on it its name. The village is in a valley presenting wild and picturesque scenery. The supply of the mineral water is abundant; and the quality resembles that of the White Sulphur springs, 22 m. to the E of this place.
BLUFF, a village in Mercer co., in the state of Illinois, U. S., 142 m. NNW of Springfield. BLY
BLUFF DALE, a village in Greene co., in Illinois, 78 m. SW of Springfield.

BLUFF ISLAND, an island on the S side of the estuary of the Cameroon's river, on the SW coast of Africa, in N lat. 3° 7', E long. 9° 20'.—Also an island lying off the W coast of the island of Palawan, in the Chinese sea, in N lat. 10° 40′, E long. 119° 20′.
BLUFF POINT, or Breaker's Point, the NW end of Sandalwood island, in S lat. 9° 42'.
BLUFFTON, a town in Wells co., in the state of Iowa, U. S., 107 m. NNE of Indianapolis.
BLUMBERG. See BLOMBERG.
BLUMECK, or Blumegg, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lake, 10 m. NNW of Schaffhausen. Pop. 288. Gypsum and alabaster are wrought near this place.
BLUMENAU, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Königsberg, SW of Holland.
BLUMENFELD, a bailiwick and v. of Baden, in the circle of the Lake, 24 m. WNW of Constanz. Pop. of v. 227.

BLUMENSTEIN, a parish and v. in Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, 6 m. W of Thun, at the foot of the Stockhorn, on the Fallbach. Pop. 862. There are mineral springs in the neighbourhood.—Also a v. of Kur-Hessen, in Lower Hessen, in the bailiwick of Rotenburg.
BLUMENTHAL, a village of Hanover, in the duchy of Bremen, 12 m. NW of Bremen, near the r. bank of the Weser. Pop. 282.-Also a v. of Hanover, W of Stade. Pop. 351.

BLUMLIS ALP, a portion of the Helvetic Alps, to the S of the lake of Lucerne, lying between the canton of Berne and the Valais. Its flanks are covered with enormous glaciers; and it attains an alt. of 12,168 ft.
BLUNDESTON, a parish in Suffolk, 3\frac{1}{2} m. NW of Lowestoft. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 592.
BLUNHAM, a parish in Bedfordshire, 5 m. NNW of Biggleswade, on the W bank of the Ivel. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 1,050.
BLUNSDON (BROAD), a chapelry in Highworth p., in Wilts, 4 m. W by S of Highworth. Pop. 831.
BLUNSDON (St. Andrew), a parish in Wilts, 5 m. WSW of Highworth. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 79.
BLUNTISHAM, a parish and township in Huntingdonshire, 5 m. NE by E of St. Ives, on the W bank of the Ouse. Area 3,410 acres. Pop. 1,457.
BLURTON AND LIGHTWOOD-FOREST, a chapelry in the p. of Trentham, in Staffordshire, 5 m. SE by E of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Area 2,210 acres. Pop. 876.
BLYA, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Friesland, 6 m. NW of Dokkum, near the coast of the Wadden entrance to the Zuyder-zee.
BLYBOROUGH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 9 m. NE of Gainsborough. Area 3,080 acres. Pop. 197.
BLYMHILL, a parish in Staffordshire, 6 m. WNW of Brewood. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. 633.
BLYSSOOG, a river of Wales, rising in Pembrokeshire, and falling into the Tivy 5 m. SE of Cardigan.

BLYTH, or BLITHE, a river of Staffordshire, which rises to the E of Henley, and flows SSE 24 m. into the Trent, which it joins 5 m. below Rudgeley. -Also a river of Northumberland, rising in the central high lands of the co., and flowing E 20 m. into the German ocean.-Also a river of Suffolk, rising near Laxford, and flowing E into the German ocean, near Southwold. It has a course of 16 m., and is navigable to Halesworth, a distance of 8 m.
BLYTH, a parish and v. in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area of p. 17,070 acres. Pop. 3,488.—The v. is 6 m. from East Retford, and locally in Nottinghamshire. Pop. 758. It has a fine church.
BLYTHBURGH, a parish and v. in Suffolk, 4 m. W of Southwold. Area of p. 3,590 acres. Pop. 837.
BLYTHE, a township in Marion co., in the state of Arkansas, U.S. Pop. 275.—Also a township in Caldwell co., in Missouri. Pop. 410.

BLYTHE (South), a township and port in Northumberland, in Earsdon p., 9 m. SE of Morpeth. Pop. in 1801, 1,170; in 1841, 1,774. About 100 small vessels belong to this port, which has a good harbour, and a lighthouse in N lat. 55° 7', W long. 1° 30′. The chief exports are coal and salt.
BLYTHFORD, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. E by S of Halesworth, on the N bank of the Blyth. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 223.
BLYTON, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. NE of Gainsborough. Area 2,830 acres. Pop. 647.
BNIN, or BNIALY, a town of Prussia, in the duchy of Posen, 12 m. NNE of Schrimm. Pop. in 1837, 1,153.
BO ISLANDS, or Hod Islands, a group off the SE extremity of Gilolo island, in S lat. 1° 12′, E long. 129° 10'. They are inhabited; and afford cocoanuts, and salt and dried fish.
BOA, a village of Norway, in the prov. of Christiansand, 20 m. S by W of Stavanger.
BOAD, or Bodha, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, on the S bank of the Mahanuddi, near the pass into the Cuttach district.
BOADILLA-DE-RIO-SECO, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. NNW of Valladolid, on the Sequillo.
BOAL. See Loango.
BOALSBURG, a village in Centre co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 84 m. NW of Harrisburg, on the main branch of Spring creek.
BOALZO, a village of Lombardy, in the Valteline, near the Adda, 2 m. SW of Tirano.
BOA-MORTE, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the l. bank of the Rio Paraupeba, 15 m. ENE of the town of Queluz.
BOANES, a native tribe of Indians, in Buenos Ayres, located to the W of the Sierra d'Uruguay, between the river of that name and the Rio Negro.
BOARDMAN, a town in Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, 10 m. SE of Warren, 127 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 929.
BOARHUNT, a parish in Southamptonshire, 2 m. NNE of Fareham. Area 1,850 acres. Pop. 232.
BOAS, a town of Austria, in the Siebenbrugen, NW of Mediasch.

BOASSICA, or BOACICA, a lake of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, between the Rio-das-Ostras and the Macahe, near the coast. It is about 4,300 yards in length, and 1,050 yards in its greatest breadth; is of great depth, and abounds in fish. Several small streams flow into it, the largest of which gives name to the lake.
BOASSU, a small river of Brazil, in the district of San Gonçalo, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro. Its mouth forms a small port of difficult access.
BOATIANG, a town of Cambodia, on the l. bank of the Meinam, 150 m. N of Panompeng.
BOAT ISLAND, an island off the coast of Labrador, in the gulf of St. Lawrence, in N lat. 50°, W long. 60° 50'.
BOA-VIAGHEM, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara.—Also a fortified islet in the bay of Rio-de- Janeiro.

BOA-VISTA, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, on the Tocantins.—Also a v. in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, on the l. bank of the Jatuba.—Also a v. in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the Sierra da Montiqueira; and another on the r. bank of the Rio de San Francisco.—Also a mountain-ridge in Brazil, branching off from the cordillera Hibiappaba, and ション・ファン・ファン・ファン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラン・プラ BOB separating the provinces of Ceara and Piauhi.—Also a mountain in the prov. of Sergipe, rising in the centre of a plain, on the r. bank of the Rio Real.—Also a v. on the S bank of the Rio Negro, 10 leagues above San Isabel.

BOA-VISTA, or BONAVISTA, one of the Cape Verd islands, in N lat. 16° 7', W long. 22° 55′ 34". It is low, rocky, and mountainous; and of nearly octagonal outline, measuring 10 m. across. Pop. 9,000. It produces cotton, indigo, and sugar; but salt is the principal article of export.
BOBADILLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. NW of Malaga, on the r. bank of the Guadajos.
BOBALO (EL), a village of Spain, in the prov. and 23 m. NNW of Madrid, near the l. bank of the Manzanares.
BOBBI, or Bobbio, a parish and village of Piedmont, in the valley of Lucerne. It occupies a highly elevated site, and is embosomed in hills, and overhung by vast mountain-peaks.
BOBBING, a parish in Kent, 2 m. W by N of Milton. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. 404.
BOBBINGTON, a parish in Salop, 8 m. SW of Wolverhampton. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 418.
BOBBINGWORTH, a parish in Essex, 3 m. NW of Chipping-Ongar. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 357.

BOBBIO, a prov. and town of Sardinia, in the gov. of Genoa. Pop. of p. in 1821, 31,354. It is divided into 27 com., and has an area of about 300 sq. m.—The cap., of the same name, is situated on the Trebbia, at the foot of the Apennines, 45 m. NE of Genoa. Pop. 4,000. It is the see of a bishop.
